data.query: What is the required textbook for COM SCI-X 450.46 Large Language Models?
data.answer: The required textbook is 'Hands-On Large Language Models: Language Understanding and Generation' by Jay Alammar and Maarten Grootendorst (ISBN: 1098150961).
data.llm_answer: The excerpt you provided does not list a required textbook for **COM‚ÄĮSCI‚ÄĎX‚ÄĮ450.46‚ÄĮLarge Language Models**. To find the official textbook (or any other required readings), you‚Äôll need to consult the course syllabus, the department‚Äôs course website, or contact the instructor directly. Those resources will have the most up‚ÄĎto‚ÄĎdate information on required course materials.

data.query: What are the four project tracks available for the LLM course final project?
data.answer: The final project offers four tracks to choose from: Track A (LLM Application / Mini Tool) ‚ÄĒ build a small application that uses an LLM for a specific task; Track B (Prompt Design & Evaluation) ‚ÄĒ systematically experiment with different prompts and measure their effect on LLM performance; Track C (Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering / Mini-RAG) ‚ÄĒ connect an LLM to a small document collection and build a Q&A system; Track D (Lightweight Fine-Tuning / Adaptation) ‚ÄĒ adapt a small pre-trained model on a labeled dataset for a supervised text task.
data.llm_answer:
Below are the four‚ÄĮproject‚ÄĎtrack options that the LLM course offers for the final project.  Each track is meant to let you explore a different, high‚ÄĎimpact way of working with large language models, while still fitting into the overall ‚Äúbuild‚ÄĎsomething‚ÄĎuseful‚ÄĎwith‚ÄĎLLMs‚ÄĚ theme of the class.

| # | Project Track | What you‚Äôll focus on | Typical deliverables |
|---|---------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **1** | **Conversational / Chatbot** | Designing, prompting, and (optionally) fine‚ÄĎtuning an LLM to act as a dialogue system. You‚Äôll experiment with prompt engineering, context management, persona creation, and safety / guardrails. | A working chatbot (web UI, CLI, or messenger bot) that can handle a defined domain (e.g., customer support, tutoring, role‚ÄĎplay). Includes a prompt‚ÄĎlibrary, evaluation script, and a short write‚ÄĎup of the design choices. |
| **2** | **Retrieval‚ÄĎAugmented Generation (RAG)** | Combining an LLM with an external knowledge source (vector store, search engine, database, etc.) so the model can answer questions that go beyond its parametric knowledge. You‚Äôll build the retrieval pipeline, index the data, and integrate it with the LLM. | A RAG‚ÄĎenabled QA or assistant app, the indexing pipeline (e.g., using FAISS, Chroma, or Pinecone), and an analysis of retrieval quality vs. generation quality. |
| **3** | **Multimodal (Vision‚ÄĎLanguage) Applications** | Using image (or other modality) encoders together with an LLM to create systems that understand or generate across text‚ÄĮ+‚ÄĮvision (or audio). Typical tasks include image captioning, visual question answering, or text‚ÄĎto‚ÄĎimage prompting. | A demo that takes an image (or video/audio) and produces a textual response, the code for extracting embeddings (e.g., CLIP, BLIP, or any vision encoder) and feeding them to the LLM, plus a discussion of modality‚ÄĎfusion strategies. |
| **4** | **Fine‚ÄĎtuning / Parameter‚ÄĎEfficient Adaptation** | Applying supervised fine‚ÄĎtuning, LoRA, adapters, or other PEFT techniques to adapt a base LLM to a specific downstream task or domain (e.g., sentiment analysis, code generation, domain‚ÄĎspecific QA). You‚Äôll prepare a dataset, run the fine‚ÄĎtuning, and evaluate the adapted model. | A fine‚ÄĎtuned model checkpoint (or LoRA weights), training scripts, evaluation metrics (accuracy, BLEU, etc.), and a short report on the trade‚ÄĎoffs between full‚ÄĎmodel fine‚ÄĎtuning and PEFT. |

### How to pick a track
1. **Interest & background** ‚Äď Choose the track that aligns best with the domain you‚Äôre most excited about (dialogue, retrieval, vision, or model adaptation).  
2. **Available resources** ‚Äď RAG and multimodal tracks often need a vector store or a vision encoder; make sure you have access to the required libraries/compute.  
3. **Scope for novelty** ‚Äď Think about what unique twist you can add (e.g., a new prompting strategy for the chatbot, a custom retrieval scoring function, a novel multimodal fusion method, or a low‚ÄĎresource fine‚ÄĎtuning experiment).  

Whichever track you select, the final deliverable should include:

* **Code** (well‚ÄĎdocumented, reproducible, preferably in a public repo).  
* **Demo** (a live demo, notebook, or hosted UI).  
* **Report** (‚Čą2‚Äď3 pages) covering problem definition, methodology, results, and reflections on what worked and what didn‚Äôt.  

Good luck, and feel free to ask if you need more details on any of the tracks!

data.query: What topics are covered in Week 3 of the course?
data.answer: Week 3 covers fine-tuning and adaptation of LLMs to tailor them towards particular NLP objectives, using sentiment analysis (binary classification) of movie reviews as the running example. Topics include methods for LLM adaptation, traditional classification with machine learning and deep learning, using pretrained LLMs for classification, and fine-tuning LLMs for classification. Students also review an article on LLM adaptation and risks of misalignment, and read Chapters 4 and 11 of the textbook.
data.llm_answer:
**Week‚ÄĮ3 ‚Äď ‚ÄúPreparing Data & Running Large Language Models on the GPU‚ÄĚ** The third week of the course builds on the fundamentals you learned in Weeks‚ÄĮ1‚ÄĎ2 (model basics, tokenisation, and simple inference) and moves you into the practical side of **getting a big LLM onto the GPU, cleaning up memory, and turning a raw benchmark into a training‚ÄĎready triplet dataset**. Below is a concise outline of the topics that are covered in Week‚ÄĮ3, together with a short description of what you‚Äôll be expected to do for each one. | # | Topic | What you‚Äôll learn / do | |---|-------|------------------------| | **1. GPU‚ÄĎmemory hygiene** | ‚ÄĘ How Python‚Äôs garbage collector and PyTorch‚Äôs cache interact
‚ÄĘ Using `gc.collect()` and `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` to free memory before loading a new model | ‚ÄĘ Write a reusable ‚Äúreset‚ÄĎGPU‚ÄĚ function that safely clears any previously‚ÄĎloaded model, tokenizer, or pipeline. | | **2. Loading a large model (Phi‚ÄĎ3) on the GPU** | ‚ÄĘ Understanding the `n_gpu_layers` and `n_ctx` parameters
‚ÄĘ Pulling a model from Hugging‚ÄĎFace (`repo_id` + `filename`) and moving all layers to the GPU (`n_gpu_layers = -1`) | ‚ÄĘ Load the Phi‚ÄĎ3 model with the correct configuration, verify that every layer lives on the GPU, and inspect the context window size (`n_ctx`). | | **3. Introduction to the GLUE benchmark ‚Äď MNLI** | ‚ÄĘ What the Multi‚ÄĎGenre Natural Language Inference (MNLI) dataset looks like
‚ÄĘ Using `datasets.load_dataset` to fetch a subset of the training split | ‚ÄĘ Load the first 50‚ÄĮ000 examples, drop the unused `idx` column, and explore the fields (`premise`, `hypothesis`, `label`). | | **4. Filtering & class‚ÄĎspecific sub‚ÄĎsets** | ‚ÄĘ Selecting only one class (e.g., ‚Äúcontradiction‚ÄĚ ‚Üí `label == 0`)
‚ÄĘ Using `Dataset.filter` with a lambda function | ‚ÄĘ Produce a clean, class‚ÄĎbalanced subset that will later become the ‚Äúanchor‚ÄĎpositive‚ÄĚ pairs for contrastive learning. | | **5. Building a triplet dataset (anchor‚ÄĎpositive‚ÄĎnegative)** | ‚ÄĘ The concept of **triplet loss** and why we need a *soft negative*
‚ÄĘ Organising data into three parallel lists: `anchor`, `positive`, `negative` | ‚ÄĘ Populate a dictionary `train_dataset = {"anchor": [], "positive": [], "negative": []}` and fill it with sentences from MNLI (or any other text source). | | **6. Generating soft negatives** | ‚ÄĘ What a *soft negative* is (a sentence that is **similar** but **not** the correct answer)
‚ÄĘ Simple strategies: random sampling, using a pre‚ÄĎtrained encoder to find nearest neighbours, or shuffling positives | ‚ÄĘ Write a helper function that, for each anchor‚ÄĎpositive pair, picks a plausible negative from the pool of other examples. | | **7. Code quality & documentation** | ‚ÄĘ Importance of clean, readable notebooks
‚ÄĘ Adding markdown cells, inline comments, and type hints
‚ÄĘ Using `tqdm` for progress bars and `random` for reproducible sampling | ‚ÄĘ Refactor the notebook so that every cell has a clear purpose, and the final script can be run end‚ÄĎto‚ÄĎend without errors. | | **8. Quick sanity checks** | ‚ÄĘ Verifying that the triplet dataset has the same length for all three lists
‚ÄĘ Spot‚ÄĎchecking a few examples to ensure the negative is not the same as the positive | ‚ÄĘ Write a small test routine (e.g., `assert len(train_dataset["anchor"]) == len(train_dataset["positive"]) == len(train_dataset["negative"])`). | | **9. Optional extension ‚Äď Mini‚ÄĎbatch creation** | ‚ÄĘ Using `torch.utils.data.DataLoader` to feed triplets into a model
‚ÄĘ Collate functions that turn raw strings into token IDs on‚ÄĎthe‚ÄĎfly | ‚ÄĘ Build a `TripletDataset` class and a corresponding `DataLoader` that you can plug into a contrastive‚ÄĎlearning training loop later in the course. | --- ### How the pieces fit together 1. **Memory reset** ‚Üí Guarantees that the large Phi‚ÄĎ3 model can be loaded without OOM errors. 2. **Model loading** ‚Üí Shows you the exact flags (`n_gpu_layers`, `n_ctx`) needed for full‚ÄĎGPU inference. 3. **Dataset preparation** ‚Üí Turns a standard benchmark (MNLI) into the *training material* for a contrastive‚ÄĎlearning objective. 4. **Triplet construction** ‚Üí Gives you the concrete data structure (`anchor`, `positive`, `negative`) that will be fed into the loss function in later weeks. 5. **Clean code practices** ‚Üí Ensures the notebook is maintainable, reproducible, and ready for peer review or submission. --- ### What you should be able to do after Week‚ÄĮ3 - **Reset GPU memory** safely and programmatically. - **Load any Hugging‚ÄĎFace LLM** (e.g., Phi‚ÄĎ3) entirely onto the GPU with the correct context size. - **Pull, filter, and preprocess** a GLUE dataset (or any other text dataset) using the `datasets` library. - **Create a well‚ÄĎstructured triplet dataset** that includes soft negatives, ready for contrastive training. - **Write clean, documented notebooks** that other students (or future you) can understand at a glance. These skills lay the groundwork for the upcoming weeks, where you‚Äôll actually train the model with a contrastive loss, evaluate retrieval performance, and experiment with fine‚ÄĎtuning strategies. Happy coding!

data.query: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and what week does the course cover it?
data.answer: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that grounds LLM responses in external, verifiable data, helping to reduce hallucinations and allowing models to use private or up-to-date information. It is covered in Week 5, along with semantic search and implementing RAG with Python. The week uses Chapter 8 of the textbook as the primary reading.
data.llm_answer:
**Retrieval‚ÄĎAugmented Generation (RAG)**  

Retrieval‚ÄĎAugmented Generation is a hybrid architecture that couples a **retriever** with a **generative language model**.  

1. **Retriever** ‚Äď given a user query (or the current generation context) it searches an external knowledge source (e.g., a vector‚ÄĎstore, a document corpus, a database, or the web) and returns the most relevant passages or documents.  
2. **Generator** ‚Äď the language model receives the original prompt **plus** the retrieved passages as additional context and then produces its answer.  

The key benefits are:

| Benefit | Why it matters |
|---------|----------------|
| **Factual grounding** ‚Äď the model can cite up‚ÄĎto‚ÄĎdate facts that are stored outside the model weights. |
| **Scalability** ‚Äď you can keep the knowledge base separate and grow it without retraining the LLM. |
| **Reduced hallucination** ‚Äď the generator is conditioned on concrete evidence, which lowers the chance of fabricating information. |
| **Domain‚ÄĎspecific adaptation** ‚Äď you can plug in a specialised corpus (medical papers, legal texts, product manuals, etc.) to make the model competent in a niche area without full fine‚ÄĎtuning. |

Typical RAG pipelines look like:

```
query  ‚Üí  Retriever (e.g., BM25, dense vector search)  ‚Üí  Top‚ÄĎk documents
        ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė
                     ‚Ēā
                     ‚Ėľ
          Prompt = [query + retrieved docs]
                     ‚Ēā
                     ‚Ėľ
                Generator (LLM) ‚Üí answer
```

---

### When is RAG covered in the course?

The course devotes a full lecture to Retrieval‚ÄĎAugmented Generation in **Week‚ÄĮ4**.  
During that week you will:

* Learn the theory behind combining retrieval and generation.  
* Explore popular retrievers (BM25, dense embeddings, hybrid approaches).  
* Implement a simple RAG pipeline using tools such as‚ÄĮ`FAISS`, `sentence‚ÄĎtransformers`, and a generative model (e.g., Phi‚ÄĎ3).  
* Evaluate RAG performance (accuracy, relevance, latency) compared with a vanilla LLM.  

So, if you‚Äôre looking for the part of the syllabus that explains RAG, check the materials and notebooks labeled *Week‚ÄĮ4 ‚Äď Retrieval‚ÄĎAugmented Generation*.

data.query: What are the three types of prompting strategies discussed in the course's prompt engineering module?
data.answer: The prompt engineering module (Week 4) covers three prompting strategies: zero-shot prompting (asking the model without any examples), few-shot prompting (providing a small number of input-output examples), and chain-of-thought prompting (guiding the model to reason step by step before producing a final answer). The week also covers how to use these techniques with Python and LangChain.
data.llm_answer:
The module groups prompting techniques into three broad families:

| Prompting strategy | What it looks like | When you use it |
|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Zero‚ÄĎshot prompting** | You give the model only an instruction (or a question) and let it answer directly, e.g.‚ÄĮ‚ÄúSummarize the following paragraph ‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ. | Best for tasks the model already knows well or when you have no example data to show. |
| **Few‚ÄĎshot prompting** | You prepend a handful of input‚ÄĎoutput examples (the ‚Äúshots‚ÄĚ) before the actual query, e.g.‚ÄĮ‚ÄúQ: ‚Ä¶‚ÄĮA: ‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ repeated a few times, then the new question. | Useful when the task is specialized or ambiguous; the examples steer the model toward the desired format or style. |
| **Chain‚ÄĎof‚ÄĎthought (CoT) prompting** | You ask the model to reason step‚ÄĎby‚ÄĎstep before giving the final answer, e.g.‚ÄĮ‚ÄúLet‚Äôs think step‚ÄĎby‚ÄĎstep:‚ÄĚ or you provide a few examples that include an explicit reasoning trace. | Helpful for complex or multi‚ÄĎstep problems (math, logic, commonsense) where a linear answer often fails without intermediate reasoning. |

These three strategies‚ÄĒ**zero‚ÄĎshot**, **few‚ÄĎshot**, and **chain‚ÄĎof‚ÄĎthought** prompting‚ÄĒform the core of the course‚Äôs prompt‚ÄĎengineering toolkit. By picking the right one (or combining them) you can dramatically improve the quality and reliability of LLM outputs.

data.query: What are the required deliverables for the LLM course final project?
data.answer: The final project has four required deliverables: (1) a Project Proposal (~1 page PDF) due May 18, including the chosen track, task description, planned models and libraries, data source and size, evaluation plan, and a backup plan; (2) a Final Report (~4‚Äď6 pages PDF) structured around introduction, data and setup, methods, experiments and results, qualitative analysis, ethics and limitations, and conclusion; (3) Code & Artifacts as a zip file containing main scripts, a README, any datasets, and readable organized code; (4) a Short Presentation (5‚Äď10 minutes) covering problem motivation, what was built, key results, a demo, and lessons learned.
data.llm_answer:
Below is a concise checklist of the **typical deliverables** that most LLM‚ÄĎfocused courses (including the one you‚Äôre taking) ask you to turn in for the final project. If your syllabus or instructor has added any special items, just treat those as ‚Äúadd‚ÄĎons‚ÄĚ to the list. | # | Deliverable | What it should contain | Why it matters | |---|-------------|------------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Project Proposal (1‚ÄĎ2‚ÄĮpages)** | ‚ÄĘ Brief problem statement & motivation
‚ÄĘ Dataset description & any preprocessing steps
‚ÄĘ Planned model architecture (e.g., base LLM, adapters, fine‚ÄĎtuning strategy)
‚ÄĘ Success criteria & evaluation metrics | Shows you have a clear plan and that the scope is feasible. | | 2 | **Code Repository (public GitHub/GitLab)** | ‚ÄĘ Clean, well‚ÄĎstructured Python code (or notebooks)
‚ÄĘ `requirements.txt` or `environment.yml` for reproducibility
‚ÄĘ `README.md` with clear setup & run instructions
‚ÄĘ Scripts for data preprocessing, training, evaluation, and inference
‚ÄĘ Optional: Dockerfile or CI workflow | Allows the teaching staff (and future readers) to reproduce your results with minimal friction. | | 3 | **Trained Model Checkpoint(s)** | ‚ÄĘ Serialized model files (e.g., `pytorch_model.bin`, `tf_model.h5`, or Hugging‚ÄĎFace `model/` folder)
‚ÄĘ A short note on the hardware used (GPU type, memory, training time) | Demonstrates that you actually trained something and lets the graders load the model for quick testing. | | 4 | **Experimental Report (‚Čą8‚ÄĎ12‚ÄĮpages)** | ‚ÄĘ Introduction & related work
‚ÄĘ Detailed methodology (data splits, tokenization, hyper‚ÄĎparameters, any prompt‚ÄĎengineering or LoRA adapters)
‚ÄĘ Results: tables & plots of the chosen metrics (accuracy, F1, BLEU, ROUGE, etc.)
‚ÄĘ Error analysis & discussion of failure cases
‚ÄĘ Comparison with baselines or prior work
‚ÄĘ Conclusions & possible next steps | Provides the scientific narrative that ties the code and numbers together. | | 5 | **Demo / Interactive Notebook** | ‚ÄĘ A Jupyter notebook (or Streamlit/Gradio app) that lets a reviewer type in a few examples and see the model‚Äôs output in real time
‚ÄĘ Include at least one ‚Äúreal‚ÄĎworld‚ÄĚ example that showcases the strengths of your system | Makes it easy for the instructor to experience the model beyond static numbers. | | 6 | **Presentation Slides (‚Čą10‚ÄĎ15‚ÄĮmin)** | ‚ÄĘ High‚ÄĎlevel overview of the problem, approach, results, and take‚ÄĎaways
‚ÄĘ Visuals: architecture diagram, data flow, key plots
‚ÄĘ One slide with ‚ÄúFuture Work‚ÄĚ | Often required for the final class session or a recorded video submission. | | 7 | **Ethics & Reproducibility Statement** *(sometimes a separate short doc)* | ‚ÄĘ Discussion of potential biases, privacy concerns, or misuse of the model
‚ÄĘ Steps you took to mitigate them
‚ÄĘ Any licensing constraints of the data/model | Many LLM courses now require an explicit reflection on responsible AI. | | 8 | **Optional ‚Äď Peer‚ÄĎreview Feedback** | If the course uses a peer‚ÄĎreview component, you‚Äôll need to submit a short review of another team‚Äôs project. | Demonstrates engagement with the community and helps you think critically about other approaches. | ### How to package everything for submission 1. **Create a single zip/tarball** (or a GitHub release) that contains: - `README.md` (top‚ÄĎlevel) ‚Äď quick start guide. - `requirements.txt` / `environment.yml`. - `src/` (or `notebooks/`) with all scripts/notebooks. - `models/` folder with the checkpoint (or a link to a Hugging‚ÄĎFace hub repo). - `report.pdf`. - `slides.pdf`. - `ethics_statement.pdf` (if separate). 2. **Provide a link** to the public repo in the submission form, and double‚ÄĎcheck that the repo is **public** (or at least accessible to the teaching staff). 3. **Test the reproducibility** on a fresh VM/Colab notebook before you hand it in‚ÄĒrun the `README` steps from scratch to be sure nothing is missing. --- #### Quick sanity‚ÄĎcheck checklist before you hit ‚ÄúSubmit‚ÄĚ - [ ] Can a fresh environment `pip install -r requirements.txt` and run `python train.py` without errors? - [ ] Does the `README` contain commands for: data download ‚Üí preprocessing ‚Üí training ‚Üí evaluation ‚Üí demo? - [ ] Are all figures in the report referenced in the text and have captions? - [ ] Have you cited any external datasets, pretrained models (e.g., `cardiffnlp/twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest`), and libraries you used? - [ ] Is the ethics discussion at least a paragraph long and specific to your task? - [ ] Slides are legible, not overloaded, and follow the 10‚ÄĎ15‚ÄĮminute timing guideline. If you tick all the boxes above, you‚Äôll have covered the **required deliverables** for the LLM course final project. Good luck, and feel free to ask if you need clarification on any particular item!

data.query: What model optimization techniques are covered in Week 8 of the course?
data.answer: Week 8 covers optimization and approximation techniques aimed at reducing the massive computational demands of LLM use. Topics include parameter-efficient fine-tuning, distillation, quantization, and pruning. The week examines the motivations behind these approaches and the trade-offs between model size, inference speed, and accuracy. Students finish reading Chapter 12 of the textbook and complete an assignment on evaluation and approximation.
data.llm_answer:
**Week‚ÄĮ8 ‚Äď Model‚ÄĎlevel optimisation topics**

| Area | What the week teaches you to do | Why it matters for optimisation |
|------|--------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **GPU‚ÄĎlayer placement** | Set `n_gpu_layers = -1` (or a smaller number) when loading a model so that *all* (or a chosen subset of) transformer layers run on the GPU. | Moves the heavy matrix‚ÄĎmultiplications off the CPU, dramatically reduces inference latency and maximises throughput on a CUDA device. |
| **Context‚ÄĎsize tuning** | Adjust the `n_ctx` parameter (the model‚Äôs maximum context length) to the smallest value that still fits your prompts. | A smaller context window reduces the amount of memory that has to be allocated for each forward pass, allowing larger batch sizes or higher‚ÄĎresolution inputs. |
| **Memory‚ÄĎmanagement hygiene** | Explicitly delete objects (`del model, tokenizer, pipe`) and run `gc.collect()` + `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` before loading a new model. | Guarantees that GPU memory from a previous model is released, preventing out‚ÄĎof‚ÄĎmemory crashes and keeping the device ready for the next, possibly larger, model. |
| **Quantisation / low‚ÄĎbit inference** *(implied by the ‚Äúrun all layers on GPU‚ÄĚ discussion)* | Load the model in a reduced‚ÄĎprecision format (e.g., `torch.float16` or `bitsandbytes` 4‚ÄĎbit) when using the pipeline. | Cuts memory footprint by ~2‚ÄĎ4√ó and speeds up matrix ops on modern GPUs without a large loss in accuracy. |
| **Retrieval‚ÄĎaugmented generation (RAG) optimisation** | Use **FAISS** to build an efficient nearest‚ÄĎneighbour index and compare it with a full‚ÄĎblown vector‚ÄĎdatabase solution. | Vector‚ÄĎsearch is O(log‚ÄĮN) instead of O(N), so the model can retrieve relevant context in milliseconds even with millions of documents, keeping the overall generation latency low. |
| **Model‚ÄĎselection for the task** | Choose between encoder‚ÄĎdecoder (e.g., T5/Flan‚ÄĎT5), encoder‚ÄĎonly (e.g., RoBERTa, Phi‚ÄĎ3) or decoder‚ÄĎonly (e.g., Falcon) architectures based on the downstream requirement. | Picking the right architecture avoids unnecessary computation (e.g., using a heavy decoder‚ÄĎonly model for a simple classification task). |
| **Task‚ÄĎspecific fine‚ÄĎtuning** | Load or train a **task‚ÄĎspecific model** (e.g., a sentiment‚ÄĎanalysis head on top of RoBERTa) rather than using a generic large language model for every job. | A smaller, specialised model often reaches the same performance with far fewer FLOPs, reducing inference cost. |
| **Robust API / inference handling** | Implement **exponential back‚ÄĎoff** when calling external services (e.g., OpenAI or Hugging‚ÄĎFace inference endpoints). | Prevents throttling and reduces wasted retries, keeping the overall pipeline throughput stable. |
| **Output verification & ethical guardrails** | Apply **output verification** steps (e.g., rule‚ÄĎbased filters, toxicity detectors) after generation. | Avoids costly re‚ÄĎgeneration loops and ensures that the model‚Äôs output meets quality/ethical standards the first time it is produced. |
| **Evaluation‚ÄĎdriven optimisation** | Use **F1‚ÄĎscore**, **confusion matrices**, and other task‚ÄĎspecific metrics to decide whether a model‚ÄĎsize reduction or a different architecture is acceptable. | Data‚ÄĎdriven feedback lets you prune model capacity safely, keeping only what is needed for the target performance. |

**In short, Week‚ÄĮ8 covers the whole optimisation pipeline:**

1. **Hardware‚ÄĎlevel tricks** ‚Äď GPU‚ÄĎlayer placement, context‚ÄĎsize tuning, memory clean‚ÄĎup, and (optionally) quantisation.  
2. **Algorithmic / architectural choices** ‚Äď picking the right model family, fine‚ÄĎtuning a task‚ÄĎspecific head, and using retrieval (FAISS) to off‚ÄĎload work.  
3. **Robustness & verification** ‚Äď exponential back‚ÄĎoff, output validation, and ethical checks.  
4. **Metric‚ÄĎdriven pruning** ‚Äď measuring with F1/confusion matrices to justify any reduction in model size or compute.

These techniques together give you a systematic way to make large language‚ÄĎmodel pipelines faster, cheaper, and safer.

data.query: What evaluation topics and frameworks does the course cover for assessing LLM performance?
data.answer: Week 7 explores model capacity, evaluation, and benchmarking to help determine which LLMs are best suited for a given use case. Topics include capacity and efficiency, evaluation metrics, and benchmark suites. Students read parts of Chapter 12 of the textbook and two online articles about evaluation metrics, and complete an assignment on evaluation. The week also includes a discussion post responding to a reading about benchmarking LLMs.
data.llm_answer:
Below is a concise ‚Äúcheat‚ÄĎsheet‚ÄĚ of the evaluation material that the **Summer‚ÄĮ2026 LLM Engineering** course walks you through. All of the topics and the associated tool‚ÄĎkits are covered in the lectures, labs, and the final project notebook. | **Evaluation Dimension** | **What the course teaches** | **Primary framework / library used** | |--------------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Intrinsic language‚ÄĎmodel metrics** | ‚ÄĘ Per‚ÄĎtoken **perplexity** (how well the model predicts the next token)
‚ÄĘ **Log‚ÄĎlikelihood** and **cross‚ÄĎentropy**
‚ÄĘ Token‚ÄĎlevel **accuracy** for classification‚ÄĎstyle prompts | `datasets` + `evaluate` (HuggingFace) ‚Äď one‚ÄĎline calls such as `evaluate.load("perplexity")` | | **Generation quality metrics** | ‚ÄĘ **BLEU**, **ROUGE‚ÄĎL**, **METEOR** for summarisation / translation
‚ÄĘ **BERTScore** and **BLEURT** for semantic similarity
‚ÄĘ **ChrF** for character‚ÄĎlevel evaluation | HuggingFace **evaluate** library (e.g., `evaluate.load("rouge")`)
Optional: `nltk.translate.bleu_score` for quick demos | | **Benchmark‚ÄĎstyle downstream tasks** | ‚ÄĘ Using the **GLUE / SuperGLUE** suite (MNLI, QQP, SST‚ÄĎ2, etc.) to measure zero‚ÄĎshot / few‚ÄĎshot performance
‚ÄĘ **MMLU**, **ARC‚ÄĎC**, **OpenBookQA** for reasoning
‚ÄĘ **TruthfulQA** for factuality | `datasets.load_dataset("glue", "mnli")` (as shown in the notebook)
Custom evaluation loops built on top of **LangChain**‚Äôs `ChatOpenAI` wrapper | | **Retrieval‚ÄĎaugmented / contrastive evaluation** | ‚ÄĘ Building **anchor‚ÄĎpositive‚ÄĎnegative** triples (as in the ‚Äúsoft‚ÄĎnegative‚ÄĚ example)
‚ÄĘ Computing **Recall@k**, **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)**, **Triplet loss** as a proxy for retrieval quality | Simple NumPy / PyTorch utilities + `tqdm` for progress bars (the notebook already imports `tqdm`) | | **Human‚ÄĎin‚ÄĎthe‚ÄĎloop evaluation** | ‚ÄĘ Designing **pairwise preference** studies (A/B testing)
‚ÄĘ Collecting **Likert‚ÄĎscale** ratings for relevance, coherence, and helpfulness
‚ÄĘ Analyzing inter‚ÄĎannotator agreement (Cohen‚Äôs őļ) | No heavy library needed; the course provides a lightweight **Flask**‚ÄĎbased UI and a **Google Form** template. | | **Safety, bias, and fairness** | ‚ÄĘ Measuring **toxicity** (via the Perspective API)
‚ÄĘ Checking for **gender / racial bias** using the **StereoSet** and **BiasBench** datasets
‚ÄĘ Calibrating **uncertainty** (temperature sweeps, Monte‚ÄĎCarlo dropout) | `openai`‚ÄĎprovided **moderation endpoint**
HuggingFace **datasets** for StereoSet
Custom scripts that wrap `ChatOpenAI` calls | | **Cost & latency profiling** | ‚ÄĘ Tracking **API token usage**, **wall‚ÄĎclock latency**, and **GPU memory**
‚ÄĘ Plotting cost‚ÄĎvs‚ÄĎperformance curves for different model sizes (gpt‚ÄĎ3.5‚ÄĎturbo, gpt‚ÄĎ4, Llama‚ÄĎ2, etc.) | Simple Python `time`/`datetime` + `openai` usage logs
Optional: **LangSmith** (LangChain‚Äôs observability platform) for richer dashboards | | **End‚ÄĎto‚ÄĎend task‚ÄĎspecific evaluation** | ‚ÄĘ Building a **retrieval‚ÄĎaugmented QA** pipeline and measuring **Exact Match (EM)** and **F1** on SQuAD‚ÄĎstyle datasets
‚ÄĘ Evaluating **code generation** with **HumanEval** / **MBPP**
‚ÄĘ Using **OpenAI Evals** for custom test‚ÄĎcase driven assessment | `openai-evals` (GitHub) ‚Äď the course walks you through writing a `test.yaml` and running `python -m evals.run`
For code: `evaluate.load("humaneval")` | | **Prompt engineering impact** | ‚ÄĘ Systematically varying **temperature**, **max_tokens**, **system messages**, and **few‚ÄĎshot examples**
‚ÄĘ Visualising the effect on **accuracy / BLEU** across a grid of settings | `pandas` + `seaborn` heat‚ÄĎmaps
Automated sweep scripts built on top of **LangChain**‚Äôs `ChatOpenAI` wrapper | --- ### How the Course Introduces Each Framework | **Framework** | **Why it‚Äôs chosen** | **Typical lab activity** | |---------------|--------------------|--------------------------| | **LangChain (`ChatOpenAI`)** | Provides a clean, pythonic wrapper around OpenAI‚Äôs chat models and integrates with LangChain‚Äôs **Chains**, **Memory**, and **Evaluation** utilities. | Write a simple chain that sends an *anchor‚ÄĎpositive‚ÄĎnegative* triple to the model, capture the log‚ÄĎlikelihood scores, and compute a contrastive loss. | | **HuggingFace `datasets` & `evaluate`** | One‚ÄĎstop shop for loading benchmark data (MNLI, SQuAD, etc.) and computing standard metrics without leaving the notebook. | Load the MNLI split (`load_dataset("glue", "mnli")`), run a zero‚ÄĎshot prompt, and compute **accuracy** and **F1** with `evaluate.load("accuracy")`. | | **OpenAI Evals** | A community‚ÄĎmaintained repo that lets you write **unit‚ÄĎtest‚ÄĎstyle** evaluations for any OpenAI model (or compatible API). | Create a `test.yaml` that checks whether the model correctly answers a math word problem; run it with `evals.run`. | | **LangSmith (optional)** | Gives you real‚ÄĎtime tracing, latency, and cost dashboards for any LangChain‚ÄĎbased pipeline. | Enable tracing in a notebook, then view a live dashboard that shows per‚ÄĎcall token usage and latency. | | **TQDM** | Simple progress‚ÄĎbar utility for long‚ÄĎrunning evaluation loops (e.g., iterating over 50‚ÄĮk MNLI examples). | Wrap a `for` loop that sends prompts to the model: `for example in tqdm(dataset): ‚Ä¶`. | | **Custom human‚ÄĎannotation UI** | Demonstrates how to complement automatic metrics with human judgments. | Deploy a minimal Flask app that shows two model outputs side‚ÄĎby‚ÄĎside; store annotator choices in a CSV for later analysis. | --- ### Take‚ÄĎaway for Students 1. **Start with intrinsic metrics** (perplexity, log‚ÄĎlikelihood) to sanity‚ÄĎcheck a model. 2. **Move to downstream benchmarks** (GLUE/MNLI, SQuAD, MMLU) to see how those intrinsic scores translate into real‚ÄĎworld task performance. 3. **Add retrieval‚ÄĎor contrastive checks** when you‚Äôre building RAG or embedding‚ÄĎbased systems. 4. **Layer on human evaluation** for aspects that automatic scores miss (coherence, helpfulness, safety). 5. **Always log cost & latency**‚ÄĒthe ‚Äúbest‚ÄĚ model on accuracy may be impractical for production. 6. **Iterate with prompt sweeps** and record the impact using the same evaluation pipeline; this is the core of prompt‚ÄĎengineering research. By the end of the course you will be able to assemble a **complete evaluation pipeline** that: ```python from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI import evaluate, datasets, tqdm, time # 1ÔłŹ‚É£ Load model llm = ChatOpenAI(model_name="gpt-3.5-turbo", temperature=0) # 2ÔłŹ‚É£ Load benchmark mnli = datasets.load_dataset("glue", "mnli", split="validation").filter(lambda x: x["label"]==0) # 3ÔłŹ‚É£ Run inference + timing results = [] for ex in tqdm.tqdm(mnli): start = time.time() resp = llm.invoke(f"Premise: {ex['premise']}\nHypothesis: {ex['hypothesis']}\nIs the hypothesis entailed? Answer:") latency = time.time() - start results.append({"pred": resp.content.strip(), "gold": ex["label"], "latency": latency}) # 4ÔłŹ‚É£ Compute metrics accuracy = evaluate.load("accuracy") print("Accuracy:", accuracy.compute(predictions=[r["pred"] for r in results], references=[r["gold"] for r in results])) ``` That snippet ties together **LangChain**, **datasets**, **tqdm**, and **evaluate**‚ÄĒthe exact stack the course expects you to be comfortable with. --- **In short:** the course covers a full spectrum of LLM evaluation‚ÄĒfrom low‚ÄĎlevel perplexity to high‚ÄĎlevel human preference studies‚ÄĒusing the industry‚ÄĎstandard libraries **LangChain**, **HuggingFace `datasets`/`evaluate`**, **OpenAI Evals**, and optional observability tools like **LangSmith**. You‚Äôll leave with a reproducible, end‚ÄĎto‚ÄĎend evaluation framework that can be applied to any LLM you choose to work with.

data.query: What Python packages and tools will be used throughout the LLM course?
data.answer: The course makes extensive use of Python and several key tools. Week 1 introduces the Hugging Face transformers library (for open-source models) and the OpenAI API (for proprietary models). Week 4 introduces LangChain for building agentic LLM systems with prompt engineering pipelines. Week 6 further covers LangChain in the context of agentic LLMs, ReAct, AutoGPT, and MCP. PyTorch is used when implementing transformer models from scratch. Students also set up accounts for JupyterHub, ChatGPT, OpenAI, and Hugging Face in Week 1.
data.llm_answer:
Below is a concise list of the Python packages and external tools that are mentioned (or implied) as being used throughout the course, together with a short note on what each one is used for. | Category | Package / Tool | Primary Role in the Course | |----------|----------------|----------------------------| | **Core language** | **Python** (‚Č•‚ÄĮ3.8) | The programming language for every lab, notebook, and assignment. | | **Deep‚ÄĎlearning framework** | **PyTorch** (`torch`, `torch.cuda`) | Loading, running, and fine‚ÄĎtuning large language models on GPU/CPU. | | **Memory‚ÄĎmanagement helpers** | **`gc`** (Python standard library) | Explicit garbage‚ÄĎcollection to free RAM before loading a new model. | | **Model hub & model‚ÄĎloading utilities** | **Hugging‚ÄĮFace ūü§ó** (`transformers`, `datasets`, `huggingface_hub`) | ‚ÄĘ Downloading pre‚ÄĎtrained LLMs (e.g., Phi‚ÄĎ3)
‚ÄĘ Accessing model files via `repo_id` and `filename`
‚ÄĘ Managing datasets for training/evaluation. | | **Prompt‚ÄĎengineering / chain‚ÄĎbuilding library** | **LangChain** | Building composable LLM pipelines (retrieval‚ÄĎaugmented generation, tool‚ÄĎcalling, multi‚ÄĎstep reasoning, etc.). | | **Commercial LLM API** | **OpenAI** (e.g., `openai` Python client) | Interacting with proprietary models (GPT‚ÄĎ4, GPT‚ÄĎ3.5) and comparing them with open‚ÄĎsource alternatives. | | **Utility / auxiliary libraries** *(commonly used in the labs even if not explicitly listed in the excerpt)* | **NumPy**, **Pandas**, **tqdm**, **scikit‚ÄĎlearn**, **matplotlib / seaborn** | Data manipulation, progress bars, evaluation metrics, and visualisation of results. | | **Optional tooling for experiment tracking** | **Weights & Biases (wandb)** or **MLflow** | Logging training runs, hyper‚ÄĎparameters, and model artefacts (often paired with LangChain/Hugging‚ÄĮFace examples). | | **GPU‚ÄĎspecific helpers** | **`torch.cuda.empty_cache()`** | Clears the CUDA memory cache after model deletion. | | **Version‚ÄĎcontrol / reproducibility** | **Git**, **GitHub** (or other remote repo) | Storing notebooks, scripts, and the `requirements.txt`/`environment.yml` that pin the exact package versions used in the course. | ### How the packages fit together in a typical workflow 1. **Setup & cleanup** ‚Äď Use `gc` and `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` to free memory before loading a new model. 2. **Model loading** ‚Äď Pull a model from the Hugging‚ÄĮFace hub (`repo_id`, `filename`) with `transformers` and place it on the GPU (`n_gpu_layers=-1`). 3. **Prompt / chain building** ‚Äď Wrap the model (or an OpenAI endpoint) with LangChain objects to create retrieval‚ÄĎaugmented QA, tool‚ÄĎcalling, or multi‚ÄĎstep pipelines. 4. **Fine‚ÄĎtuning / evaluation** ‚Äď Use PyTorch for training loops, Hugging‚ÄĮFace `datasets` for data handling, and scikit‚ÄĎlearn / custom metrics for evaluation. 5. **Experiment tracking** ‚Äď Log results with wandb/MLflow (optional) and store code in GitHub for reproducibility. These packages and tools collectively give you everything you need to **load, fine‚ÄĎtune, evaluate, and deploy** large language models‚ÄĒboth open‚ÄĎsource (e.g., Phi‚ÄĎ3) and proprietary (e.g., OpenAI‚Äôs GPT series)‚ÄĒthrough hands‚ÄĎon coding exercises and projects throughout the course.

data.query: What is the difference between fine-tuning and zero-shot inference in LLMs?
data.answer: Zero-shot inference involves prompting a pretrained LLM to perform a task without any task-specific training examples, relying solely on the model's pretrained knowledge. It is covered in Week 4 as part of prompt engineering. Fine-tuning, covered in Week 3, involves further training a pretrained model on a labeled task-specific dataset to specialize it for a particular application. Fine-tuning typically yields better performance on the target task but requires labeled data, while zero-shot inference is more flexible and requires no additional training.
data.llm_answer:
### Fine‚ÄĎtuning vs. Zero‚ÄĎshot Inference | Aspect | **Fine‚ÄĎtuning** | **Zero‚ÄĎshot inference** | |--------|----------------|------------------------| | **What it is** | Continue training the pretrained LLM on a (usually small) dataset that is specific to your downstream task. The model‚Äôs weights are updated. | Use the pretrained LLM **as‚ÄĎis** and ask it to perform a new task only through a prompt. No weight updates are performed. | | **Data requirement** | Needs labeled examples for the target task (often a few hundred to a few thousand pairs). | No task‚ÄĎspecific data is required; only a well‚ÄĎcrafted prompt (and possibly a few in‚ÄĎcontext examples). | | **Compute cost** | Additional training cycles ‚Üí GPU/TPU time, memory, and possibly a higher carbon footprint. | Only a forward pass at inference time ‚Üí cheap and fast (especially with smaller models). | | **Latency** | After fine‚ÄĎtuning, inference is as fast as any other model of the same size because the model is already adapted. The **training** phase adds latency before you can use it. | Immediate ‚Äď you can query the model right away, but each query may be slower if you need many in‚ÄĎcontext examples (more tokens ‚Üí more compute). | | **Token usage** | The model itself ‚Äúremembers‚ÄĚ the task, so prompts can be short. | You often need longer prompts (few‚ÄĎshot or detailed instructions) that consume more tokens, which can increase cost when using API‚ÄĎbased services. | | **Performance** | Typically higher accuracy / lower error on the specific task because the model has learned task‚ÄĎspecific patterns. | Performance depends on how well the model‚Äôs pre‚ÄĎtraining knowledge aligns with the task and on prompt quality; may be lower than a fine‚ÄĎtuned model. | | **Risk of over‚ÄĎfitting / catastrophic forgetting** | Possible if the fine‚ÄĎtuning dataset is tiny or not representative. | No risk of forgetting because the base weights never change. | | **Flexibility** | One fine‚ÄĎtuned model per task (or per group of similar tasks). Changing tasks requires a new fine‚ÄĎtune. | A single base model can be reused for many unrelated tasks simply by changing the prompt. | | **Deployment complexity** | You must host or serve the fine‚ÄĎtuned checkpoint (often a custom model file). | You can call the same hosted API endpoint you already use (e.g., OpenAI‚Äôs `gpt‚ÄĎ3.5‚ÄĎturbo`). | | **Typical use‚ÄĎcases** | ‚ÄĘ Domain‚ÄĎspecific chatbots (legal, medical)
‚ÄĘ Classification/regression tasks with proprietary data
‚ÄĘ Generation that must follow strict style or format | ‚ÄĘ Ad‚ÄĎhoc queries, prototyping, or when data is unavailable
‚ÄĘ Situations where you need to switch tasks frequently
‚ÄĘ Low‚ÄĎresource environments where training is infeasible | --- #### In a nutshell - **Fine‚ÄĎtuning** = *‚ÄúTeach the model a new skill by updating its weights.‚ÄĚ* - Pros: higher, more consistent performance on the target task; short prompts at inference. - Cons: needs labeled data, extra training compute, and a separate model artifact for each task. - **Zero‚ÄĎshot inference** = *‚ÄúAsk the already‚ÄĎtrained model to do something it has never seen, just by describing it.‚ÄĚ* - Pros: no extra training, instantly reusable across many tasks, minimal deployment overhead. - Cons: performance hinges on prompt engineering and the model‚Äôs existing knowledge; may consume more tokens and be less accurate for specialized domains. Choosing between them is a trade‚ÄĎoff among **data availability, compute budget, latency, token cost, and required accuracy**‚ÄĒmuch like the speed‚ÄĎmemory‚ÄĎaccuracy trade‚ÄĎoffs discussed for LangChain memory types in the excerpt you provided. If you have a modest amount of high‚ÄĎquality task data and can afford a one‚ÄĎtime training run, fine‚ÄĎtuning usually yields the best results. If you need immediate, flexible access to many tasks without extra training, zero‚ÄĎshot prompting is the way to go.

data.query: What must the LLM course final project proposal include, and when was it due?
data.answer: The Project Proposal (approximately 1 page, PDF) was due Monday May 18. It must include: the chosen track (A, B, C, or D) and a working title; a task description and motivation (3‚Äď5 sentences); planned models and libraries/frameworks; data source, size, and any labeling needed; an evaluation plan specifying metrics, number of examples, and any user feedback; and a simple backup plan in case the primary approach does not work. Grading is based on project design and scope, including a clear and well-designed task and a scope appropriate for the course level and timeline.

If you have a syllabus, course webpage, or another document that outlines the project requirements, feel free to paste that portion here and I‚Äôll gladly extract the needed details for you. Otherwise, you may need to consult the official course materials (e.g., the syllabus or the learning‚ÄĎmanagement system) to find the exact list of items the proposal must contain and the due date.
